name: Twenty Items of Knowledge — Amanitvadi
proposition: True knowledge consists of twenty virtues beginning with humility (amānitva), non-hypocrisy, non-harm, patience, uprightness — these are the conditions for jñāna, not its content
repetition_rationale: First and only full enumeration of these twenty virtues as jñāna-conditions; the BG treats these as a unified set
echo_hypothesis: Search for amānitva, adhyātmanityatva, viviktadevasevitva as a cluster — any MBH sage or description using this vocabulary is echoing this node

name: Brahman as the Knowable — Anadi Para Brahma
proposition: Brahman is the object of knowledge — beginningless, called neither sat nor asat, pervading all, the light of lights, seated in the heart of all
repetition_rationale: Unique to ch13 — the fullest description of Brahman as the object of knowledge (jñeya), with paradoxical attributes
echo_hypothesis: Search for jñeya + brahma, anādi + brahma, jyotiṣām jyotiḥ — sages describing the knowable absolute in paradoxical terms

name: Purusha-Prakriti Duality — Both Beginningless
proposition: Purusha and Prakriti are both beginningless; Prakriti is the cause of effects and instruments, Purusha is the experiencer of pleasure and pain
repetition_rationale: The purusha-prakriti framework appears in ch3 and ch7 but ch13 gives the fullest Sāṃkhya-inflected account, with the witness-self portrait (13.22-23) as a sub-claim
echo_hypothesis: Search for puruṣa + prakṛti + anādi, bhoktṛtva paired with kartṛtva — Sāṃkhya passages in Śāntiparva and Anugītā will echo this

name: Liberation Through Vision — Seeing the Same Lord in All
proposition: One who sees the supreme Lord dwelling equally in all perishable beings, the imperishable within the perishable — that one truly sees
repetition_rationale: Restates N09 (same self in all) from a different angle — the vision of the imperishable in the perishable; the non-doer in the doer; these are three distinct but related perceptions converging on liberation
echo_hypothesis: Search for paśyati + samaṃ + sarvabhūteṣu, or the sky/ākāśa metaphor (like space not being stained) — this simile appears in Upaniṣads and may recur in MBH

name: Brahman as the Womb of All Beings
proposition: The great Brahman is the womb; the Lord places the seed in it — all beings are born from this union of the field and the knower
repetition_rationale: Unique cosmogonic image — Brahman as the cosmic womb, Ishvara as the father-seed — establishes the origin of diversity from a single source
echo_hypothesis: Search for mahad brahma + yoni, bīja + garbha in cosmogonic contexts — creation hymns and Brahma-creation narratives in Ādi and Śāntiparva

name: How Each Guna Binds the Embodied Self
proposition: Sattva binds through attachment to happiness and knowledge; rajas binds through attachment to action; tamas binds through delusion, sloth, and sleep
repetition_rationale: While N06 names the three gunas, this node is a distinct claim: the mechanism of bondage differs for each guna; even sattva binds
echo_hypothesis: Search for nibadhnāti + guṇa, sattvena badhnāti — Sāṃkhya expositions in Śāntiparva on how the gunas produce bondage

name: Cosmic Destinations by Dominant Guna at Death
proposition: At death, if sattva dominates one goes to higher worlds; if rajas, one is reborn among action-attached; if tamas, one is born in deluded wombs
repetition_rationale: The eschatological corollary of guna theory — where you go depends on which guna predominates at the moment of death; unique to ch14
echo_hypothesis: Search for ūrdhvaṃ gacchanti sattvasthāḥ + adho gacchanti tāmasāḥ — this trisection of cosmic destinations appears in Sāṃkhya contexts in Śāntiparva

name: Portrait of the Gunatita — Beyond the Three Qualities
proposition: One who neither hates illumination, activity, and delusion when present, nor desires them when absent, who is indifferent like a witness — that one has transcended the gunas
repetition_rationale: Parallel to the sthitaprajña portrait (N05) but now from the guna-framework angle; distinct because the criterion is not-hating-not-desiring the three qualities themselves
echo_hypothesis: Search for guṇātīta + udāsīna, sarvārambhaparityāgī — any sage described as unmoved by the three qualities, described from this vocabulary

name: Bhakti as the Vehicle Beyond the Gunas
proposition: By unswerving devotion one transcends the three gunas and becomes fit for Brahman — Brahman itself is the ground of the immortal, the eternal, the everlasting happiness
repetition_rationale: Ch14 closes with an unexpected move — after the entire guna analysis, it is bhakti (not jñāna or sāṃkhya) that lifts one beyond; unique synthesis here
echo_hypothesis: Search for bhaktiyogena guṇātīta — devotion framed as the means of transcending the guna-structure; rare formulation to track in MBH

name: The Cosmic Ashvattha Tree — World as Inverted Tree
proposition: The cosmic tree has its roots above and branches below — its leaves are the Vedas; one who knows it is a Veda-knower; cut it with the axe of non-attachment
repetition_rationale: The inverted tree is a unique cosmological image in BG — roots above = Brahman, branches below = manifest world; the whole world is a proliferating tree that must be cut
echo_hypothesis: Search for aśvattham + ūrdhvamūlam — this specific image appears in KaṭhaU 2.3.1 and may recur in Śāntiparva cosmological sections

name: The Jiva as Eternal Fragment of the Divine
proposition: The living soul in the world of life is an eternal fragment of Me — it draws to itself the mind and the five senses dwelling in Prakriti
repetition_rationale: Unique claim: the individual jīva is not separate from but is a fragment (aṃśa) of the divine — this is the metaphysical basis for devotion and oneness simultaneously
echo_hypothesis: Search for mamaivāṃśo jīvaloke — this exact phrase or its paraphrase; also jīvabhūta + sanātana in contexts of individual-divine relationship

name: The Lord as the Inner Power in All — Vaisvanara
proposition: The Lord enters the earth and sustains beings; becomes the fire (Vaiśvānara) in the body and digests the four kinds of food; enters the heart as memory and knowledge
repetition_rationale: Distinct from the antaryāmin node (N31): here the emphasis is on the Lord's identity with elemental cosmic functions — earth-sustainer, fire-digester, vedic knower — not just the inner witness
echo_hypothesis: Search for vaiśvānara + deha, smṛtir jñānam apohanaṃ ca — the Lord as digester and as the source of memory/forgetting; tracks across hymns to Viṣṇu/Brahman in MBH

name: Three Purushas — Kshara, Akshara, Purushottama
proposition: Two purushas exist in the world — the perishable (all beings) and the imperishable (the immovable kūṭastha); the highest is a third, the Purushottama who pervades and sustains all three worlds
repetition_rationale: This node refines N16 — while N16 names purushottama, this node provides the full tripartite ontology: kṣara (manifold becoming), akṣara (immovable being), and Purushottama (the transcendent who surpasses both)
echo_hypothesis: Search for kṣaraḥ sarvāṇi bhūtāni + kūṭastho 'kṣara — and the beyond-both formulation; Anugītā and Śāntiparva Sāṃkhya sections will deploy this triad

name: The Daivi Sampad — Divine Qualities as Liberation's Equipment
proposition: Fearlessness, purity of being, steadiness in jñāna-yoga, charity, self-restraint, sacrifice, study, austerity, uprightness — these constitute the divine estate that leads to liberation
repetition_rationale: While N30 contrasts daivi/āsuri natures, this node specifically maps the daivī sampad as soteriological equipment — a positive list of twenty-six virtues; it echoes the amānitva list of ch13 (N36) but extends it
echo_hypothesis: Search for abhayaṃ sattvasaṃśuddhi + daivīm — also any enumeration of virtue lists in MBH where sages describe the qualities of the good; these recur in Anuśāsanaparva

name: The Three Gates of Hell — Kama Krodha Lobha
proposition: Desire, anger, and greed are the three gates of hell — the destruction of the self; abandoning these three, one should act for one's own good
repetition_rationale: While N12 covers kāma as root of bondage, this is a distinct claim: the triple-gate formula — kāma-krodha-lobha as the three specific portals to self-destruction; this triad appears repeatedly in MBH
echo_hypothesis: Search for kāmaḥ krodhas tathā lobhas — this exact triad is one of the most grep-stable clusters; it appears verbatim in Śāntiparva and Anuśāsanaparva ethical sections

name: Shastra as the Authority for Action
proposition: One who abandons the injunction of śāstra and acts from personal desire attains neither perfection nor happiness nor the highest goal — let śāstra be your authority for what is to be done
repetition_rationale: Unique node: BG explicitly validates śāstra as the epistemological standard for action — this is the only place it makes this meta-claim about scriptural authority
echo_hypothesis: Search for śāstraṃ pramāṇaṃ — this formulation or śāstravidhim utsṛjya as a negative marker; dharmaśāstra-oriented passages in Anuśāsanaparva will echo this

name: Shraddha Traya — Three Types of Faith
proposition: Faith is of three kinds born of each person's own nature — the sattvic worship the devas, the rajasic worship yakṣas and rākṣasas, the tamasic worship pretas and bhūtas
repetition_rationale: The claim that a person is constituted by their śraddhā (yo yacchraddhāḥ sa eva saḥ) is among the most psychologically profound in BG; the tripartition by guna then applies this to worship
echo_hypothesis: Search for śraddhāmayo 'yaṃ puruṣaḥ — this is the most quotable formulation; also śraddhā + trividhā + svabhāvajā; track in texts on religious duty and devotional practice

name: Three Types of Tapas — Bodily, Speech, Mental
proposition: Austerity is of three kinds: of body (reverence, purity, non-harm), of speech (true, pleasant, beneficial words, recitation), and of mind (serenity, gentleness, silence, self-restraint, purity of intention)
repetition_rationale: While tapas is mentioned often in BG, this is the only systematic threefold analysis of tapas by mode (body/speech/mind) and by guna quality (sāttvika/rājasa/tāmasa)
echo_hypothesis: Search for śārīraṃ tapa + vāṅmayaṃ tapa + mānasam — this classification recurs in Anuśāsanaparva on tapas; also manaḥprasāda + saumyatva + mauna as a cluster

name: Three Types of Dana — Sattvic Rajasic Tamasic
proposition: Sattvic giving is to one who cannot repay, in the right place, time, and to a worthy person; rājasic giving expects return; tāmasic giving is done with contempt in wrong circumstances
repetition_rationale: The three-guna analysis of dāna is unique to ch17; emphasizes context (deśa-kāla-pātra) as constitutive of the quality of giving
echo_hypothesis: Search for dānaṃ + deśa + kāla + pātra — this tetrad appears in dharmaśāstra and is echoed in Anuśāsanaparva chapters on dānadharma

name: OM TAT SAT — Three Designations of Brahman
proposition: OM, TAT, SAT are the three-fold designation of Brahman from which all rites, Vedas, and brahmins were instituted; actions without śraddhā are called asat and are naught in this world or the next
repetition_rationale: Unique ritual-ontological node: OM TAT SAT as three names of Brahman; the chapter ends with the negative — action without śraddhā is asat, real neither here nor hereafter
echo_hypothesis: Search for oṃ tat sad iti nirdeśo — this exact phrase; also aśraddhayā hutaṃ dattaṃ as the negative marker; track in ritual and Brahman-naming contexts across MBH

name: Sannyasa vs Tyaga — Resolution of the Distinction
proposition: Sannyāsa is the abandonment of desire-motivated actions; tyāga is the abandonment of the fruit of all actions; the tyāgī performs yajña-dāna-tapas without attachment to fruit — this is sattvic tyāga
repetition_rationale: Ch18 opens with the question Arjuna posed at the start — the BG's entire arc resolves here; this node is the final answer to N10 (tyāga vs sannyāsa), now with a clear verdict: sattvic tyāga = perform duty, drop the fruit
echo_hypothesis: Search for kāmyānāṃ karmaṇāṃ nyāsaṃ + sarvakarmaphalatyāgam — the technical pairing of sannyāsa and tyāga definitions; also phalatyāgī as a descriptor in MBH ethical passages

name: Five Causes of All Action — The Samkhya Account
proposition: All actions are accomplished by five causes: the locus (body), the agent, the instruments, the various operations, and the fifth — fate (daiva); one who sees only the self as agent is of perverted understanding
repetition_rationale: Unique Sāṃkhya-inflected analysis of action causation; the five-cause theory distributes agency and undoes the ego's claim to sole authorship; daiva as the fifth cause is crucial
echo_hypothesis: Search for pañcaitāni kāraṇāni + adhiṣṭhānaṃ tathā kartā — this exact enumeration; Sāṃkhya discussions in Śāntiparva on the causes of action will echo this

name: Knowledge Karma Karta in Three Gunas
proposition: Knowledge, action, and the agent are each of three kinds according to the gunas: sattvic knowledge sees one imperishable in all; sattvic action is obligatory and non-attached; the sattvic doer is free of ego and fortitude
repetition_rationale: The triplication of jñāna-karma-kartā by gunas is ch18's systematic exposition; it operationalizes the guna framework (N06) into the three fundamental categories of every action
echo_hypothesis: Search for jñānaṃ karma ca kartā ca tridhaiva guṇabhedataḥ — this exact formula or its paraphrase in Sāṃkhya-Yoga expositions; also muktasaṅgo 'nahaṃvādī as a quality-descriptor

name: Buddhi and Dhrti in Three Gunas
proposition: Intellect (buddhi) that knows pravṛtti and nivṛtti, what is to be done and not done, bondage and liberation — that is sattvic; dhṛti that maintains mind, prāṇa, and senses by yoga without wavering — that is sattvic
repetition_rationale: Unique to ch18 — the application of the three-guna framework to the faculties of intellect and resolve; distinct from knowledge or action, these are the meta-capacities that govern how knowledge and action unfold
echo_hypothesis: Search for pravṛttiṃ ca nivṛttiṃ ca kāryākārye + bandhaṃ mokṣaṃ — this tetrad of what buddhi must discern; also dhṛtyā dhārayate manaḥprāṇendriyakriyāḥ

name: Three Types of Pleasure — Sattvic Pleasure Through Practice
proposition: Sattvic pleasure is like poison at first, nectar at the end — born of the clarity of self-knowledge through practice; rājasic is nectar at first and poison at the end; tāmasic deludes from beginning to end
repetition_rationale: The only explicit discussion of pleasure's quality by guna — the paradox that real happiness is hard at first; this reverses ordinary preference and gives a criterion for discriminating practice
echo_hypothesis: Search for viṣam iva pariṇāme 'mṛtopamam — the poison-nectar reversal metaphor for sattvic pleasure; also ātmabuddhiprasādajaṃ as the source of authentic happiness

name: Varnashrama as Nature-Born — Svabhava Prabhava
proposition: The duties of brāhmaṇa, kṣatriya, vaiśya, and śūdra are distributed according to their own nature-born qualities; by devotion to one's own duty one attains perfection
repetition_rationale: This node extends N13 (svadharma) by grounding it in svabhāva — one's nature-born guna configuration determines one's proper field of action; perfection is available to each through one's own domain
echo_hypothesis: Search for svabhāvaprabhavair guṇaiḥ + karmāṇi pravibhaktāni — the nature-basis for duty-distribution; also svakarmaṇā tam abhyarcya + siddhim — worship through one's own work as path to perfection

name: Naiskarmasiddhi — Perfection Through Non-Action
proposition: With unattached intellect, victorious self, gone beyond desire, one attains through renunciation the supreme perfection of non-action (naiṣkarmya)
repetition_rationale: The culminating practical path before the highest devotional teaching — naiṣkarmyasiddhi is the Vedantic goal described through the qualities of the liberated intellect; this node bridges the action discussion with the brahma-bhūta state
echo_hypothesis: Search for naiṣkarmyasiddhiṃ paramāṃ + saṃnyāsenādhigacchati — the phrase is rare and specific; also asaktabuddhi + sarvatra + jitātmā as a descriptor cluster for the renunciant

name: Brahma-Bhuta — The State of Becoming Brahman
proposition: Having become Brahman, one is serene, neither grieving nor craving, equal to all beings — in that state one attains supreme devotion to Me
repetition_rationale: The brahma-bhūta state is the pivot — not the end but the platform from which supreme bhakti arises; the sequence brahma-bhūta → parā bhakti → tattvaṃ jñātvā → viśate is the BG's final soteriological map
echo_hypothesis: Search for brahmabhūtaḥ prasannātmā + na śocati na kāṅkṣati + samaḥ sarvabhūteṣu — this triple is a complete formula; any MBH sage described in these terms is echoing this node

name: Buddhi Yoga — Surrendering All Actions to Krishna
proposition: Fix the mind on Me, take refuge in Me by buddhi-yoga; surrendering all actions to Me with mind on Me, you will cross all difficulties by My grace — if ego prevents, nature will compel
repetition_rationale: This node restates N11 (yoga as equanimity in action) but now in its most personal and theistic register — mat-para, mat-citta, mat-prasāda; also adds the counter-claim: if ego refuses, prakṛti will enforce anyway
echo_hypothesis: Search for cetasā sarvakarmāṇi mayi saṃnyasya + buddhiyogam — the combination of surrendering all actions with buddhi-yoga; also prakṛtis tvāṃ niyokṣyati as a statement about nature's compulsion

name: Ishvara as the Mover — Yantrarudhani Mayaya
proposition: The Lord dwells in the heart of all beings, causing all beings to revolve as if mounted on a machine by māyā — take refuge in Him alone with all your being
repetition_rationale: Refines N31 (Ishvara as inner ruler) by adding the mechanical metaphor — beings are puppets on a machine, moved by māyā; the response to this vision is total surrender (zaraNam), leading directly into 18.66
echo_hypothesis: Search for yantrārūḍhāni māyayā + bhrāmayan — this mechanical metaphor is striking; also hṛddeśe 'rjuna tiṣṭhati as a statement of divine immanence in the heart

name: Sarva-Dharman Parityajya — The Final Teaching
proposition: Abandon all dharmas and take refuge in Me alone — I will liberate you from all sins; do not grieve
repetition_rationale: The caramopadeśa — the final secret teaching; alone in the BG as a direct divine promise of liberation; distinct from N17 (which is the thematic node) because this node is anchored in the single verse as the BG's irreducible core
echo_hypothesis: Search for sarvadharmān parityajya + mām ekaṃ śaraṇaṃ vraja — this is one of the most-quoted BG verses; its echo in MBH would be any statement where a god or sage promises liberation to one who takes total refuge

name: Gita as Sacred Teaching — Transmission and Reception
proposition: This Gītā should not be taught to one who has no austerity, devotion, or who finds fault; one who teaches it to devotees renders the highest service and will come to Me; one who merely hears it with faith is liberated
repetition_rationale: The Gītā-māhātmya (praise and transmission rules) frames the teaching as self-referentially sacred; the BG is itself a node in the MBH — this node marks the meta-level of the BG-within-MBH structure
echo_hypothesis: Search for idaṃ te nātapaskāya — the restriction formula; also jñānayajñena + iṣṭaḥ syām as the frame of the Gītā as a jñāna-sacrifice; the BG's self-designation matters for the Ekam Sat project

name: Sanjaya's Witness — The BG as a Reported Event
proposition: Sañjaya reports: I heard this wondrous dialogue between Vāsudeva and Pārtha through Vyāsa's grace; wherever Yoga's Lord and Arjuna the archer are, there is prosperity, victory, and right conduct
repetition_rationale: The BG is embedded in the MBH through Sañjaya — this narrative frame node is essential for the Ekam Sat project: the BG is not a separate text but a discourse within the MBH, reported by a witness to Dhṛtarāṣṭra
echo_hypothesis: Search for yogeśvaraḥ kṛṣṇo yatra pārtho dhanurdharaḥ — the closing śloka is itself echoed and quoted in MBH; also sañjaya uvāca + saMvAdam as the embedded-discourse framing device
